walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Scoring Track
walkthrough.text: Let's get Quacks set up. First, place the large scoring track in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This track has numbered spaces around the outside for victory points and a row of turn spaces across the top. Place the flame marker on turn space one. This is how we will track which of the nine rounds we are on.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Seal Tiles
walkthrough.text: Find the four seal tiles, one for each player color. Place each seal tile on its matching colored space on the scoring track with the zero side face up. These tiles will flip to the fifty side if a player's score passes fifty points during the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Ingredient Books
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the ingredient books. The orange and black books are used in every game, so lay those out on the table first. For the colored books, your group needs to pick one set number from one through four. All five colored books should use the same set number. For your first game, use set one. Place only the green, blue, and red books face up for now. Keep the yellow and purple books aside because they unlock on turns two and three.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Ingredient Chips
walkthrough.text: Sort all two hundred and fifteen chips by color and place each color group near its matching ingredient book. These are the chips players will buy during the game to improve their bags. Keep them in neat piles so everyone can see what is available.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Fortune Teller Deck
walkthrough.text: Find the twenty-four Fortune Teller cards and shuffle them into a face-down deck. Place this deck near the starting player. At the beginning of each turn, you will flip the top card and follow its instructions. Purple cards resolve immediately, and blue cards last through the turn.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Rubies and Bonus Die
walkthrough.text: Place all twenty rubies in a shared supply in the middle of the table. Also find the single bonus die and keep it nearby. The player who brews farthest without exploding each round gets to roll this die for an extra reward.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player a Pot
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up each player. Give everyone one pot board in their chosen color. For your first game, use the side without the test tubes face up. The pot has a spiral track of numbered spaces starting from the center. This is where you will place your ingredient chips as you draw them from your bag.

walkthrough.title: Place the Droplet
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one droplet token and places it on the zero space in the center of their pot. This marks your starting position. As you draw and place chips, you will advance along the spiral track from this point.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Flask and Rat Stone
walkthrough.text: Each player places their flask on the large trivet space on their pot board with the full side face up. The flask lets you return one white chip to your bag once per turn, which is a lifesaver when you are close to exploding. Also place your rat stone on the small trivet to the left of the flask.

walkthrough.title: Place Scoring Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes their scoring marker and places it on the scoring track on the space matching their colored seal tile. This tracks your victory points throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Fill Your Starting Bag
walkthrough.text: Let's fill the bags. Each player takes one cloth bag in their color and adds exactly nine starting chips: four white one-chips, two white two-chips, one white three-chip, one orange one-chip, and one green one-chip. Give the bag a good shake to mix them up. These are the chips you will draw from blindly during the brewing phase.

walkthrough.title: Choose the Starting Player
walkthrough.text: The last person who cooked something goes first and holds the Fortune Teller deck. Play proceeds clockwise from there. You are all set up and ready to brew your first potion. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: The Quacks of Quedlinburg is played over exactly nine rounds. Each round, all players simultaneously draw ingredient chips from their bags and place them in their pots to brew potions. The further you push along the pot's spiral track, the more victory points and coins you earn. But if your white chips add up to more than seven, your pot explodes.

walkthrough.title: Start of Each Round
walkthrough.text: At the beginning of each round, the starting player flips the top Fortune Teller card and reads it aloud. Purple cards resolve immediately before brewing starts. Blue cards give an effect that lasts through the round. Starting from round two, the rat catch-up system also activates before brewing. We will cover that shortly.

walkthrough.title: The Rat Catch-Up System
walkthrough.text: Starting on turn two, players who are behind on the scoring track get a boost. Count the number of rat tail spaces between the leader's scoring marker and your scoring marker. Move your rat stone forward that many spaces on your pot, starting from where your droplet sits. This temporary boost gives you a head start on this round's brewing. The rat stone resets each round.

walkthrough.title: The Brewing Phase
walkthrough.text: Now everyone brews simultaneously. Reach into your bag without looking and draw one chip. Place it in your pot on the next available space after your droplet or your last placed chip. The chip's printed value tells you how far forward to place it. A one-chip goes one space ahead, a two-chip goes two spaces, and so on. Gaps between chips stay empty.

walkthrough.title: Push Your Luck
walkthrough.text: After placing each chip, you make a choice: stop or keep drawing. If you stop voluntarily, you lock in your position and wait for other players to finish. If you keep drawing, you might reach a higher scoring space, but you risk explosion. The tension is real and it is the heart of the game.

walkthrough.title: The Explosion Rule
walkthrough.text: Only white chips count toward explosion. After placing each chip, add up the values of all white chips in your pot. If the total exceeds seven, your pot explodes immediately. The exploding chip still gets placed, but you must stop drawing. Exploded players face a penalty during evaluation: they must choose either victory points or buying chips, not both.

walkthrough.title: Using Your Flask
walkthrough.text: Your flask is a one-time safety net each round. If the last chip you drew is white and it has not caused an explosion, you may flip your flask to its empty side to return that white chip to your bag. You can then continue drawing. This is incredibly useful when a white chip puts you dangerously close to the explosion threshold. The flask refills during evaluation if you land on certain spaces.

walkthrough.title: Chip Effects During Brewing
walkthrough.text: Not all chips are blank. White and orange chips have no special effect. Blue, red, and yellow chips trigger their effects immediately when drawn, based on their ingredient book's rules. Black, green, and purple chips have end-phase effects that resolve during evaluation. Always check the active ingredient books when drawing a colored chip.

walkthrough.title: Evaluation Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: Once all players have stopped or exploded, the evaluation phase begins. There are six steps resolved in order: A through F. These determine who rolls the bonus die, resolve end-phase chip effects, award rubies, grant victory points, allow chip purchases, and handle flask refills.

walkthrough.title: Evaluation Step A: Bonus Die
walkthrough.text: The player whose last chip sits on the highest-numbered scoring space, and who did not explode, rolls the bonus die. If multiple non-exploded players tie for the highest space, they all roll. The die can award victory points, rubies, a droplet advance, or a free orange chip. Exploded players never roll the bonus die.

walkthrough.title: Evaluation Steps B and C: Chip Effects and Rubies
walkthrough.text: In step B, resolve any end-phase chip effects for black, green, and purple chips based on the active ingredient books. In step C, any player whose last chip sits on a ruby space gains one ruby from the supply. Rubies are valuable because you can spend two rubies at any time to either refill your flask or advance your droplet one space.

walkthrough.title: Evaluation Steps D and E: Points and Buying
walkthrough.text: In step D, each player scores victory points equal to the number printed on the space where their last chip sits. In step E, each player spends coins equal to the coin value on that same space to buy ingredient chips. You can buy up to two chips per round, and they must be different colors. New chips go straight into your bag for future rounds. Remember, exploded players must choose either D or E, not both.

walkthrough.title: Evaluation Step F: Refill and Advance
walkthrough.text: Also resolve any droplet advancement effects. Between rounds, return all drawn chips to your bag, advance the flame marker to the next turn space, and reveal any newly unlocked ingredient books. Yellow unlocks on turn two and purple unlocks on turn three.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Spaces Explained
walkthrough.text: Each space in the pot has two values: a victory point value and a coin value. The further along the spiral you reach, the higher both values get. Some spaces also have ruby icons. The dual nature of these spaces creates a key tension because exploded players lose access to one of the two rewards.

walkthrough.title: Buying Strategy Basics
walkthrough.text: When buying chips, think about what colors synergize with your active ingredient books. Each color does something different depending on the set you are playing. Cheap synergy chips that combo with your existing bag often outperform expensive chips that do not fit your strategy. Remember, you can only buy two chips and they must be different colors.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after completing the evaluation of turn nine. Your final score is the total victory points on the scoring track. Convert remaining rubies and coins to victory points at the rulebook rate. Add any endgame bonuses from your active ingredient set. The player with the highest total wins. Ties go to the player farther along the scoring track, then to most rubies.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Brew
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with four players. Each player will draw chips, make decisions, and we will see what happens when things go right and wrong. Ready to brew some potions? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Round One Setup
walkthrough.text: It is turn one, so no rat catch-up happens yet. The starting player flips the top Fortune Teller card. Let's say it is a purple card that gives everyone one extra ruby. Each player takes a ruby from the supply. Now everyone gets ready to brew simultaneously.

walkthrough.title: Everyone's First Draw
walkthrough.text: All four players reach into their bags and draw one chip blindly. Player one draws an orange one-chip and places it one space ahead of the droplet. Player two draws a green one-chip. Player three draws a white two-chip. Player four draws a white one-chip. Everyone places their chip on the matching space in their pot.

walkthrough.title: The Second Draw
walkthrough.text: Let's keep going. Player one draws a white one-chip, placing it one space ahead. Player two draws a white two-chip. Player three draws an orange one-chip. Player four draws another white one-chip. So far, white totals are: Player one has one, Player two has two, Player three has two, and Player four has two. Everyone is safe.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Gets Greedy
walkthrough.text: Players continue drawing. Player four has been lucky and is pushing ahead on the spiral. They draw a white three-chip, bringing their white total to five. That is close to the explosion threshold of seven, but Player four wants the higher scoring space. They decide to keep drawing.

walkthrough.title: Player Four Explodes
walkthrough.text: Player four draws again and pulls out a white two-chip. Their white total jumps to seven, which is exactly the limit, so they are still safe. Feeling invincible, they draw one more time and pull a white one-chip. White total is now eight. That exceeds seven. The pot explodes! Player four must stop and will have to choose between victory points or buying chips this round, not both.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know When to Stop
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is critical. When your white total reaches five or higher, every draw becomes dangerous. Player four had five white and kept pushing. With four white one-chips, two white two-chips, and one white three-chip in your starting bag, the odds of drawing white get higher with each draw. Learn to feel the danger threshold and stop before it is too late.

walkthrough.title: Player Two Uses the Flask
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Player two is at white total six after a string of draws. They draw one more chip and it is a white two-chip, which would bring them to eight. But wait, the chip has not caused an explosion yet because it needs to be placed first. Actually, placing it brings the total to eight, which does exceed seven. Player two reaches for their flask, but the flask cannot be used on a chip that caused the explosion. Player two has exploded too.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Use Your Flask Before Explosion
walkthrough.text: Here is the key flask rule that trips up new players. You can only flask a white chip if it did not cause your explosion. If your white total was six and you drew a white two-chip, you explode at eight and the flask cannot save you. The right play would have been to flask the previous white chip when your total was still safe, or to stop drawing entirely.

walkthrough.title: Players One and Three Stop Wisely
walkthrough.text: Players one and three have been more cautious. Player one stopped after reaching a decent scoring space with a white total of four. Player three stopped at white total five after landing on a ruby space. Both players locked in solid positions without exploding.

walkthrough.title: Evaluation Begins
walkthrough.text: Now we resolve evaluation steps A through F. For step A, Player one has the highest scoring space among non-exploded players, so they roll the bonus die. They roll a ruby, nice. Step B has no end-phase chips active in round one with set one. Step C awards rubies to anyone on a ruby space, so Player three picks one up.

walkthrough.title: Points and Buying
walkthrough.text: Step D awards victory points. All four players score VP from their spaces. Step E is buying time. Players one and three can both score VP and buy chips because they did not explode. Players two and four exploded, so they had to choose. Player two chose to buy chips to improve their bag, giving up this round's VP. Player four chose VP because they landed on a high space.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Buying Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player four chose VP over buying, which means no new chips entered their bag this round. Meanwhile, Player one bought a blue two-chip and a red two-chip, making their bag stronger for future rounds. Player four will start round two with the same weak starting bag while everyone else has improved theirs.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Early Buying Beats Early Points
walkthrough.text: In the early rounds, buying chips is almost always more valuable than a few extra victory points. Your bag carries forward for all nine rounds, so every improvement compounds over time. Choosing VP over buying in round one means you fall behind on bag quality for the entire rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Cleanup and Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: Everyone returns their drawn chips to their bags and shakes them up. The flame marker advances to turn two. The yellow ingredient book is now revealed and available for the rest of the game. The rat catch-up system will activate next round, so players who fell behind on the scoring track will get a boost.

walkthrough.title: Player Four's Mistake: Ignoring Synergy
walkthrough.text: In round two, Player four finally gets to buy chips. But instead of reading the ingredient books, they just buy the most expensive chips available. One chip has a powerful effect with set three, but we are playing set one where it does almost nothing. Player four spent their coins on a chip that will not help their strategy at all.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read the Ingredient Books
walkthrough.text: Always check which ingredient set is active before buying. A cheap two-coin chip with a strong synergy in your active set will outperform an expensive four-coin chip that has no useful effect. The ingredient books are the key to smart purchases. Read them, know what each color does in your set, and buy accordingly.

walkthrough.title: How the Game Develops
walkthrough.text: As rounds progress, your bag gets better and more interesting. More colored chips mean fewer dangerous white draws and more powerful effects. The purple book unlocks on turn three, adding another layer of strategy. The scoring ramps up too, with higher spaces on the spiral becoming reachable as your bag improves.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues through all nine rounds. Each round follows the same structure: Fortune Teller card, rat catch-up, brew simultaneously, evaluate in order. The player who best balances risk-taking with smart chip purchases will come out on top. Keep an eye on your white chip density and always have a plan for your coins.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the brewing phase in action, watched two players explode, learned the flask timing rule, and discovered why early buying matters more than early points. The three big lessons: know when to stop drawing, use your flask before you explode, and always buy chips that synergize with your active ingredient set.

walkthrough.title: Before You Brew
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on when to push and when to stop.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to chip colors and evaluation steps.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun brewing!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, pushing too hard every single round. Exploding occasionally is fine, but exploding three or four times in a game cripples your scoring. Second, choosing VP over buying in the early rounds. Your bag carries forward for all nine rounds, so early investments compound enormously. Third, ignoring the ingredient books and buying chips randomly. Always know what each color does in your active set.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Odds
walkthrough.text: Your starting bag has nine chips, and seven of them are white. That means roughly seventy-eight percent of your early draws will be white. As you add colored chips, the white percentage drops. After buying two colored chips in round one, you have eleven chips with seven white, dropping to about sixty-four percent. By mid-game, a well-built bag might be only forty percent white. Track your approximate white ratio mentally to make smarter push-or-stop decisions.

walkthrough.title: The Explosion Decision
walkthrough.text: When you explode, you lose access to either VP or buying for that round. In the early game, losing buying power is devastating because it stunts your bag growth. In the late game, losing VP hurts more because the scoring spaces are worth more. A good rule of thumb: in rounds one through four, avoid explosions at almost all costs. In rounds seven through nine, controlled explosions on high-value spaces can be worth the trade-off.

walkthrough.title: Flask Timing
walkthrough.text: Your flask is your most important safety tool. Save it for when a white chip puts you near the explosion threshold, not for returning a harmless early white chip. The ideal flask moment is when you are at white total five or six and draw a white one-chip. You flask it back, keep your total manageable, and continue drawing for more valuable spaces. Using your flask on draw two of a round is almost always a waste.

walkthrough.title: Buying Priorities
walkthrough.text: Think of chip buying as investing in your future. In round one, prioritize chips that give you an immediate effect when drawn, like blue or red chips in most sets. In rounds two and three, start building toward your ingredient set's strongest combos. By mid-game, look for chips that interact with what you already have. Two rubies to advance your droplet can also be a smart use of resources if it gets you to a much better space.

walkthrough.title: The Catch-Up System
walkthrough.text: The rat catch-up mechanic is one of the most powerful systems in the game. If you are behind on the scoring track, your rat stone gives you a free head start each round. This means you can afford to play conservatively in the early rounds, focusing on buying chips over scoring VP. When your improved bag starts paying off in the mid-game, you will naturally pull ahead, and the catch-up boost shrinks as you close the gap.

walkthrough.title: Ruby Management
walkthrough.text: Rubies are surprisingly valuable. Spending two rubies to refill your flask gives you an extra safety net. Spending two rubies to advance your droplet permanently improves your starting position each round. In general, advance your droplet in the first few rounds when the permanent benefit has more time to compound. Save flask refills for the late game when you are pushing harder for VP on high-value spaces.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Planning
walkthrough.text: The game escalates rapidly in rounds seven through nine. Scoring spaces at the far end of the pot spiral are worth massive VP. At this point, your bag should be strong enough to push deep safely. If you have been buying smartly all game, your white chip density should be low. This is when you can afford to push past spaces you would have stopped at earlier. The player who builds the best bag over nine rounds almost always wins.

Don't buy chips randomly — read the ingredient books first

walkthrough.title: Bag Density Mathematics
walkthrough.text: Advanced Quacks play is all about understanding your bag composition. After each round, count your chips mentally. How many total chips do you have? How many are white? Your explosion risk per draw is your white count divided by total remaining chips. When your white percentage drops below fifty percent, you can push significantly deeper. When it is above sixty percent, respect the danger zone.

walkthrough.title: The Value Chip Strategy
walkthrough.text: Higher-value chips like three-chips and four-chips advance you further per draw, which means fewer total draws to reach high-value spaces. Fewer draws means fewer chances to hit white chips. A bag with two four-chips and three three-chips can reach space fifteen in just three or four draws, which might only encounter one or two white chips total. Compare that to needing eight draws with only one-chips.

walkthrough.title: Set-Specific Engine Building
walkthrough.text: Each ingredient set creates different optimal strategies. In some sets, green chips give massive end-phase bonuses when you have multiple greens in your pot. In others, blue chips chain together for cascading immediate effects. Before the game starts, read all the active books and identify which two or three colors have the strongest synergy. Build your purchasing plan around those colors from round one.

walkthrough.title: Controlled Explosion Strategy
walkthrough.text: Sometimes exploding on purpose is the right play. If you are already on a high-value space and the VP from that space exceeds what you could buy, taking the VP and deliberately pushing into explosion territory makes sense. This is especially powerful in the last two rounds when bag improvement no longer compounds. Just make sure the VP you gain exceeds the value of the chips you would have bought.

walkthrough.title: Droplet Advancement Optimization
walkthrough.text: Every space your droplet advances permanently improves your starting position for all future rounds. A droplet on space three means you effectively start three spaces deeper every round. Over nine rounds, that is twenty-seven free spaces of advancement. Spending rubies on droplet advancement in rounds one through three can be worth more than any chip purchase.

walkthrough.title: Fortune Teller Card Adaptation
walkthrough.text: Fortune Teller cards can dramatically shift optimal play for a round. A card that lets everyone draw an extra chip safely changes your push-or-stop math. A card that adds bonus rubies might make a ruby-space stop more valuable than pushing further. Read each card carefully and adapt your round strategy to take maximum advantage. Players who ignore Fortune Teller effects leave value on the table.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Awareness
walkthrough.text: Even though brewing is simultaneous, the scoring track matters. If you are leading, the rat catch-up system gives opponents free advancement. Sometimes it is better to score slightly less and stay closer to the pack, reducing the catch-up boost others receive. Conversely, if you are behind, the catch-up boost lets you take safer lines while still reaching competitive scoring spaces.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Push Mathematics
walkthrough.text: In rounds eight and nine, the difference between stopping at space eighteen versus pushing to space twenty-two can be ten or more victory points. At this stage, your bag should be well-built. Count your remaining white chips versus total remaining chips before each draw. If your explosion risk per draw is under thirty percent, keep pushing. The reward curve at the outer spiral vastly outpaces the risk if your bag density supports it.

summary.title: Bag Density Mathematics
bullets: White count / total chips = explosion risk per draw
Below 50% white → push deep safely
Above 60% white → respect the danger zone

summary.title: The Value Chip Strategy
bullets: High-value chips (3s, 4s) = fewer draws to reach high spaces
Fewer draws = fewer white chip encounters
3-4 high-value draws can reach space 15+

summary.title: Set-Specific Engine Building
bullets: Read all active books before game starts
Identify 2-3 colors with strongest synergy in your set
Build purchasing plan around those colors from round 1

summary.title: Controlled Explosion Strategy

Best in rounds 8-9 when bag improvement no longer compounds

summary.title: Droplet Advancement Optimization

Droplet on space 3 = 27 free spaces over 9 rounds
Ruby → droplet in rounds 1-3 can exceed chip purchase value

Adapt strategy to maximize card benefits
Ignoring Fortune Teller effects = value left on table

Under 30% explosion risk per draw → keep pushing
Outer spiral reward curve vastly outpaces risk with good bag

entries.definition: Each player's personal board with a spiral track of numbered spaces. Chips are placed on the track during brewing, advancing from the center outward.
A marker placed in the center of your pot that marks your starting position each round. Can be permanently advanced by spending rubies or through certain effects.

A one-use-per-round safety tool. If your last drawn chip is white and did not cause an explosion, flip the flask to return that chip to your bag and continue drawing. Refills during evaluation if you land on a flask space.

A catch-up marker placed ahead of your droplet at the start of each round (from round two onward). The amount equals the number of rat-tail spaces between the leader and your score marker. Resets each round.
The central board tracking victory points for all players. Also contains the turn indicator spaces for the flame marker and seal tile positions.
Tracks the current round on the turn indicator. Advances from turn one through turn nine over the course of the game.
A double-sided tile placed on the scoring track for each player. Starts on the zero side and flips to fifty when a player's score exceeds fifty points.
Reference cards that define the special effects of each colored chip. Orange and black are always in play. Green, blue, red, yellow, and purple use one of four sets chosen during setup.
A deck of twenty-four event cards. One is revealed each round before brewing. Purple cards resolve immediately; blue cards provide effects lasting through the round.
A special die rolled by the highest non-exploded player during evaluation step A. Possible rewards include VP, rubies, droplet advancement, or a free orange chip.
The dangerous chips in your bag. Their values (one, two, or three) count toward the explosion threshold. White chips have no special effect. Starting bag contains seven white chips.
Basic filler chips with no special effect. The one-value orange chip is in your starting bag. Additional orange chips can be gained from the bonus die.
Colored ingredient chips with end-phase effects that resolve during evaluation step B. Effects vary by ingredient set. The one-value green chip is in your starting bag.
Colored ingredient chips with immediate effects that trigger when drawn during brewing. Effects vary by ingredient set. Unlocked from turn one.
Colored ingredient chips with immediate effects that trigger when drawn during brewing. Effects vary by ingredient set. Unlocked from turn one.
Colored ingredient chips with immediate effects that trigger when drawn during brewing. Effects vary by ingredient set. Yellow book unlocks on turn two.
Colored ingredient chips with end-phase effects that resolve during evaluation step B. Effects vary by ingredient set. Purple book unlocks on turn three.
Colored ingredient chips with end-phase effects that resolve during evaluation step B. The black book varies by player count. Always in play.
A currency gained from ruby spaces and certain effects. Spend two rubies to either refill your flask or permanently advance your droplet one space. Remaining rubies convert to VP at game end.
Earned from your scoring space during evaluation step E. Used to buy ingredient chips. You can buy up to two chips per round, and they must be different colors.
Occurs when the total value of all white chips in your pot exceeds seven. The exploding chip is still placed. Exploded players must choose between VP (step D) or buying (step E), not both.
The magic number is seven. White chip total of seven or less is safe. White chip total of eight or more triggers an explosion. Only white chip values count toward this threshold.
The core decision each draw. Stop to lock in your current scoring space safely, or draw another chip to try to reach a higher space at the risk of explosion.
The six-step resolution after all players stop or explode. Steps A through F: Bonus Die, End-Phase Chip Effects, Rubies, Victory Points, Buy Chips, Refill and Advance.
The simultaneous draw phase where all players pull chips from their bags one at a time and place them in their pots. Continues until each player stops voluntarily or explodes.
A catch-up mechanic active from round two onward. Players behind on the scoring track move their rat stone forward by the number of rat-tail spaces between them and the leader.
One of four possible configurations for the green, blue, red, yellow, and purple ingredient books. Each set changes how those colored chips function, creating different game dynamics.
Each space in the pot spiral has two values: VP and coins. Higher spaces deeper in the spiral have better values. Some spaces also have ruby icons.
The core strategic arc of the game. By buying colored chips each round, you improve your bag's composition, reducing white chip density and adding powerful effects for future rounds.
The ratio of white chips to total chips in your bag. Lower density means safer draws and deeper pushes. A key metric for deciding when to stop or keep drawing.
When multiple chips in your bag interact with each other based on the active ingredient set. Strong synergy between colors creates more powerful effects and higher scoring potential.
